title: China Sprint: Verhaeren Dominates from Pole as Keisner Storms to Another Podium
meta: Published: 14 March 2026 | By Car Fok, Conventional Race Chronicler & Ex‑Ferrari Driver | F1CC News
caption: Philippe Verhaeren and Red Bull — sprint perfection at Shanghai.
body:
Philippe Verhaeren took his first victory of the 2026 season in the Shanghai sprint, converting pole position into a controlled, measured win by a margin of 1.466 seconds over Ferrari’s Eetu Väisänen, while Robin Keisner claimed a second consecutive sprint podium for Audi in third. The result ended Red Bull’s pointless streak from Melbourne and served notice that the team’s qualifying pace is now translating into race results, at least over the shorter format.

The sprint weekend began with a qualifying session that exposed several front‑runners to the traffic lottery of the Shanghai International Circuit’s tight first sector. In SQ1, both Mercedes and Ferrari lost one car apiece. Wesley Vandesteene, who had finished second in Melbourne, never found a clean lap on the medium compound and ended the session at the bottom of the timesheet, posting a 1:32.639 after struggling with traffic and a disrupted out‑lap sequence. Afonso Cachinho, still under scrutiny after his difficult debut in Australia, was caught in a knot of cars on his critical run and could not improve beyond a 1:32.009, which left him seventeenth and out of the session. George Konstantaras, Julian Kondra, and Richard Hicks also fell at the first hurdle, while Philippe Verhaeren, running alone in clear air, set the fastest time of SQ1. Championship leader Väisänen was compromised by traffic in sector one on his first effort and could manage only eighth, leaving himself work to do for the remainder of qualifying.

Across the ten‑minute SQ2 session, the grid compressed further. Maikel Zwart, who had appeared strong in earlier running, suffered a spin at Turn 15 that forced rivals to take evasive action and left his tyres flat‑spotted. He could not recover on his second run and was eliminated in twelfth, his 1:31.686 not enough to progress. Quarter Kevu, who had topped practice, also fell short, his time of 1:31.433 missing the cut for the top nine by two tenths. Eren Aygen, Sarah Springfield, Riley Swansson, and Sam Fakt were also knocked out, leaving a grid for SQ3 that featured both Racing Bulls and both Red Bulls, the two McLarens, Lucas Koopman’s Williams, Robin Keisner’s Audi, Michiii MaraDöner’s Alpine, and Väisänen’s Ferrari.

SQ3, with soft tyres available for the first time all session, produced a pole shootout that swung on fine margins. Lucas Koopman, the Dutchman in the Williams, laid down a lap of 1:30.757 that stood as the benchmark for several minutes. Then Philippe Verhaeren, even with a visible lock‑up, pieced together a 1:30.674 to beat Koopman by 0.083 seconds. Lukus Wright took third for Racing Bulls with a 1:30.787, and Jason Roy put the second Red Bull fourth on a 1:30.852. Zhao Lei, the home favourite, managed fifth, while Keisner, Joshua Nathanial, and Väisänen filled the next three slots, Väisänen’s 1:31.522 leaving him eighth. MaraDöner completed the top nine with a 1:31.783.

The sprint race, run over 19 laps on the medium compound, unfolded under clear skies and on a dry track. Verhaeren held the lead cleanly from the start and never looked troubled. He controlled the pace at the front, managing the gap to the cars behind and keeping his tyres in good condition throughout. Väisänen, starting eighth, began his recovery immediately. He picked off cars with decisive moves into the first sector and climbed to second place by mid‑distance, closing to within a second and a half of the leader but never mounting a serious challenge. He set the second‑fastest lap of the race, a 1:35.688, but Verhaeren had pace in reserve and crossed the line with a comfortable margin.

The performance of the race, however, came from Robin Keisner in the Audi. Starting sixth, Keisner delivered a drive full of controlled aggression, taking third place with a series of clean passes and defending the position against Lukus Wright in the closing laps. His podium added six points to the two he scored in Australia, giving Audi eight sprint points from their opening two weekends. Wright finished fourth, and Quarter Kevu took fifth after a quiet but effective afternoon, both Racing Bulls cars collecting solid points. Lucas Koopman converted his front‑row start into sixth, a respectable haul for Williams. Maikel Zwart recovered from his qualifying spin to finish seventh, while Zhao Lei took the final point in eighth for McLaren.

Further back, Jason Roy endured a difficult race after starting fourth. He dropped through the field and could only finish fifteenth, but he set the fastest lap of the sprint — a 1:34.470 — as a small consolation. Vandesteene and Cachinho, starting from the back, made limited progress, finishing seventeenth and eighteenth respectively, the damage from their qualifying exits proving impossible to undo over such a short race. Joshua Nathanial crossed the line last after a late spin cost him over half a minute.

The sprint result means Verhaeren leaves Shanghai with eight points and Red Bull’s first victory of the season. Väisänen’s seven points strengthen his championship lead, and Keisner’s third place confirms that Audi’s early‑season pace is genuine. All attention now turns to Sunday’s main race, for which the grid was set by a separate qualifying session that saw Jason Roy take pole position for Red Bull. The weekend’s biggest prize remains on the table.
